Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
Advance Machine Learning - Project
EasyVisa : visa application processing

Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Business communities in the United States are facing high demand for human resources, but one of the constant challenges is identifying and attracting the right talent, which is perhaps the most important element in remaining competitive. Companies in the United States look for hard-working, talented, and qualified individuals both locally as well as abroad.

The Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) of the US permits foreign workers to come to the United States to work on either a temporary or permanent basis. The act also protects US workers against adverse impacts on their wages or working conditions by ensuring US employers' compliance with statutory requirements when they hire foreign workers to fill workforce shortages. The immigration programs are administered by the Office of Foreign Labor Certification (OFLC).

OFLC processes job certification applications for employers seeking to bring foreign workers into the United States and grants certifications in those cases where employers can demonstrate that there are not sufficient US workers available to perform the work at wages that meet or exceed the wage paid for the occupation in the area of intended employment.

Challenge¶

In FY 2016, the OFLC processed 775,979 employer applications for 1,699,957 positions for temporary and permanent labor certifications. This was a nine percent increase in the overall number of processed applications from the previous year. The process of reviewing every case is becoming a tedious task as the number of applicants is increasing every year.

Objective¶

OFLC has hired the firm EasyVisa for data-driven solutions to handle the challenge of shortlisting candidates having higher chances of VISA approval.

  • Build a classification model to predict the likelihood of VISA approval for each applicant.

  • Identify key factors (drivers) influencing VISA certification outcomes.

  • Provide recommendations on whether a candidate’s VISA should be certified or denied based on data-driven insights.

Dataset¶

The data contains the following attributes of the applicants (employees) and their employers. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  1. case_id: ID of each visa application
  2. continent: Information of continent the employee
  3. education_of_employee: Information of education of the employee
  4. has_job_experience: Does the employee has any job experience? Y= Yes; N = No
  5. requires_job_training: Does the employee require any job training? Y = Yes; N = No
  6. no_of_employees: Number of employees in the employer's company
  7. yr_of_estab: Year in which the employer's company was established
  8. region_of_employment: Information of foreign worker's intended region of employment in the US.
  9. prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment. The purpose of the prevailing wage is to ensure that the foreign worker is not underpaid compared to other workers offering the same or similar service in the same area of employment.
  10. unit_of_wage: Unit of prevailing wage. Values include Hourly, Weekly, Monthly, and Yearly.
  11. full_time_position: Is the position of work full-time? Y = Full-Time Position; N = Part-Time Position
  12. case_status: Flag indicating if the Visa was certified or not

Importing the necessary libraries¶

In [696]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
%matplotlib inline

# Library to preprocess for label encoding
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

# To impute missing values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

# Library to split data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# To tune different models
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# Libraries to build logistic regression model
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Libraries to build decision tree classifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn import tree

# Libraries for ensemble technique : bagging
from sklearn.ensemble import BaggingClassifier, RandomForestClassifier

# Libraries for ensemble technique : boosting
from sklearn.ensemble import AdaBoostClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

# Libraries for under and oversampling
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

# To perform statistical analysis
import scipy.stats as stats

# To get diferent metric scores
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    f1_score,
    accuracy_score,
    recall_score,
    precision_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
    make_scorer,
)

# Library to suppress warnings or deprecation notes
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Loading the dataset¶

In [697]:
# load the CSV file containing data from local storage of colab notebook
application_data = pd.read_csv("EasyVisa.csv")

# copying data to another variable as backup to avoid any changes to original data
data = application_data.copy()

# get the last 10 records
data.tail(10)
Out[699]:
case_id continent education_of_employee has_job_experience requires_job_training no_of_employees yr_of_estab region_of_employment prevailing_wage unit_of_wage full_time_position case_status
25470 EZYV25471 North America Master's Y N 2272 1970 Northeast 516.4101 Hour Y Certified
25471 EZYV25472 Asia High School N N 40224 1962 Island 75587.4200 Year Y Certified
25472 EZYV25473 Asia High School N N 1346 2003 Midwest 76155.6000 Year N Certified
25473 EZYV25474 Asia Bachelor's Y N 2421 2007 Northeast 22845.5600 Year Y Certified
25474 EZYV25475 Africa Doctorate N N 2594 1979 Northeast 51104.7800 Year Y Certified
25475 EZYV25476 Asia Bachelor's Y Y 2601 2008 South 77092.5700 Year Y Certified
25476 EZYV25477 Asia High School Y N 3274 2006 Northeast 279174.7900 Year Y Certified
25477 EZYV25478 Asia Master's Y N 1121 1910 South 146298.8500 Year N Certified
25478 EZYV25479 Asia Master's Y Y 1918 1887 West 86154.7700 Year Y Certified
25479 EZYV25480 Asia Bachelor's Y N 3195 1960 Midwest 70876.9100 Year Y Certified

Observations :

  • Multiple categorical fields can be observed. They offer strong potential for segmentation and group-wise analysis, especially against the dependent variable case_status
    • continent
    • education_of_employee
    • has_job_experience
    • requires_job_training
    • region_of_employment,
    • unit_of_wage,
    • full_time_position
  • prevailing_wage (numerical) varies significantly and is tied to unit_of_wage (Hour vs. Year). This needs to be normalized for meaningful comparisons. (there is also Weekly and Monthly)
  • yr_of_estab and no_of_employees provide context on the employer and can help in correlating outcomes against the dependent variable case_status

Understand the shape of the dataset.¶

In [700]:
# get the shape of data
data.shape
Out[700]:
(25480, 12)
  • The application dataset has 25480 rows and 12 columns

Check the data types of the columns for the dataset.¶

In [701]:
# get data info
data.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 25480 entries, 0 to 25479
Data columns (total 12 columns):
 #   Column                 Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------                 --------------  -----  
 0   case_id                25480 non-null  object 
 1   continent              25480 non-null  object 
 2   education_of_employee  25480 non-null  object 
 3   has_job_experience     25480 non-null  object 
 4   requires_job_training  25480 non-null  object 
 5   no_of_employees        25480 non-null  int64  
 6   yr_of_estab            25480 non-null  int64  
 7   region_of_employment   25480 non-null  object 
 8   prevailing_wage        25480 non-null  float64
 9   unit_of_wage           25480 non-null  object 
 10  full_time_position     25480 non-null  object 
 11  case_status            25480 non-null  object 
dtypes: float64(1), int64(2), object(9)
memory usage: 2.3+ MB

Observations :

  • 9 columns are object type i.e., categorical (object data type) and 3 columns are numerical
  • Of the 9 categorical columns, has_job_experience, requires_job_training,full_time_position are boolean and can be binary encoded to 1/0
  • The categorical columnscontinent, region_of_employment, unit_of_wage, education_of_employee can be one-hot encoded
  • company's age could be derived w.r.to current year by subtracting yr_of_estab
  • case_status is the target / dependent variable and should be encoded to 1 or 0 (Certified = 1, Denied = 0)
  • case_id is not useful and could be dropped, as it serves as identifier
  • object columns can be converted to category to make the data memory-efficient and faster for analysis and to encode.

Fixing the data types¶

In [702]:
# get the object type columns
object_columns = data.select_dtypes(['object'])
object_columns.columns
Out[702]:
Index(['case_id', 'continent', 'education_of_employee', 'has_job_experience',
       'requires_job_training', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage',
       'full_time_position', 'case_status'],
      dtype='object')
In [703]:
# change the column type to category for object type columns
for i in object_columns.columns:
    data[i] = data[i].astype('category')
In [704]:
# get data info
data.info()

Statistical summary of the dataset.¶

In [705]:
# display the statistical summary of all columns
data.describe(include='all').T
Out[705]:
count unique top freq mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
case_id 25480 25480 EZYV01 1 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
continent 25480 6 Asia 16861 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
education_of_employee 25480 4 Bachelor's 10234 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
has_job_experience 25480 2 Y 14802 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
requires_job_training 25480 2 N 22525 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
no_of_employees 25480.0 NaN NaN NaN 5667.04321 22877.928848 -26.0 1022.0 2109.0 3504.0 602069.0
yr_of_estab 25480.0 NaN NaN NaN 1979.409929 42.366929 1800.0 1976.0 1997.0 2005.0 2016.0
region_of_employment 25480 5 Northeast 7195 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
prevailing_wage 25480.0 NaN NaN NaN 74455.814592 52815.942327 2.1367 34015.48 70308.21 107735.5125 319210.27
unit_of_wage 25480 4 Year 22962 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
full_time_position 25480 2 Y 22773 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
case_status 25480 2 Certified 17018 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN

Observations :

  • Top applicants who get visa certified are from Asia, with Bachelor's degree, having work experience and without the requirement for job related training
  • Maximum data appears to be available for 'year' as the Unit of wage
  • Visa applications have been for jobs that are full time and based in NorthEast of US
  • prevailing_wage has high dispersion (mean ≈ 74k, std ≈ 52k) and ranges from \$2.13 to \$319210, showing significant pay variability across roles. This is also due to the wage being provided for different unit_of_wage as in day, week, month, year
  • no_of_employees ranges from -26 (invalid) to 602,069, indicating potential data quality issues and extreme outliers.
  • has_job_experience, requires_job_training, full_time_position are booleans
  • Companies span from 1800 to 2016, with a median year of 1997, suggesting a wide mix of company ages.
  • Applicants are from 6 different continents
  • Applications are made to 5 unique regions across US
  • Education of applicants is in 4 unique categories

Note

  • As observed earlier case_id is unique and can be dropped
  • Data cleaning and careful handling of categorical and wage related variables are to be done before creating models.

Out[707]:

data.drop(["case_id"], axis=1, inplace=True)

Univariate Analysis¶

Numerical (continuous) variables and histogram¶

In [710]:
# define the plot figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

# define list of continuous numerical features to plot histogram to analyse the distribution and skewness
num_features = ['no_of_employees', 'yr_of_estab', 'prevailing_wage']

# plotting the histogram for each numerical feature
for i, feature in enumerate(num_features):
    plt.subplot(3, 1, i+1)    # assign a subplot in the main plot
    sns.histplot(data=data, x=feature)    # plot the histogram on log scale

plt.tight_layout();   # to add spacing between plots
plt.show()

Observations :

  • no_of_employees is heavily right skewed indicating most companies have a relatively small number of employees, while a few outliers have very large employee counts.
  • yr_of_estab is left skewed indicating many companies being established in recent years and a few companies are old (before 1950) and some very old (before 1850)
  • prevailing_wage is right skewed indicating most wages cluster toward the lower end, with a few employees earning significantly higher wages (in the \$150k - \$300k range)

Numerical (continuous) variables and Box plots¶

In [713]:
# define the plot figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

# plotting the boxplots for each numerical feature
for i, feature in enumerate(num_features):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i+1)    # assign a subplot in the main plot
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=feature)    # plot the boxplot

plt.tight_layout();   # to add spacing between plots
plt.show()

Observations:

  • no_of_employees : Right-skewed distribution.

    • box is very narrow and close to the lower end, while there are many extreme outliers extending far to the right.
    • This indicates, majority of companies have a small number of employees
    • Few very large companies have extremely high employee counts

  • yr_of_estab : Left-skewed distribution.

    • box is skewed to the right, with many outliers on the left (older years).
    • Most companies were established after 1950, with a heavy concentration in recent decades
    • number of older establishments exist (pre-1900) but are outliers.

  • prevailing_wage : Right-skewed distribution.

    • box is positioned toward the lower end of the range, with many outliers on the higher end.
    • Most employees receive wages in the lower-to-mid range.
    • There are numerous high-wage outliers, indicating a few high-paid roles

Overall the observations from histplot and boxplot are compimenting each other.

Category variables and Count plots¶

In [714]:
# define list of categorical features to plot countplot for analysis
# List of categorical features
cat_features = [
    'continent', 'education_of_employee', 'has_job_experience',
    'requires_job_training', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage',
    'full_time_position', 'case_status'
]

# Print feature values in percentage
for feature in cat_features:
    percentages = 100 * data[feature].value_counts(normalize=True)
    print(f"{feature}: ", end='')
    print(' | '.join([f"{idx}: {val:.1f}%" for idx, val in percentages.items()]))
print('\n')

# Plot setup: 4 rows × 2 columns = 8 subplots
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 12))
total = len(data)

for i, feature in enumerate(cat_features):
    ax = plt.subplot(3,3, i + 1)
    sns.countplot(data=data, x=feature, ax=ax)

    # Add percentage labels to each bar
    for p in ax.patches:
        height = p.get_height()
        if height == 0:
            continue
        percentage = f'{100 * height / total:.1f}%'
        label_x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2.

        if height < 0.05 * total:  # Short bars = show label above the bar
            ax.text(
                label_x,
                height + (0.01 * total),
                percentage,
                color='black',
                fontsize=9,
                ha='center'
            )
        else:  # Tall bars = show label inside with white text
            ax.text(
                label_x,
                height * 0.1,
                percentage,
                color='white',
                fontsize=9,
                ha='center'
            )

    ax.set_title(f'Distribution of {feature}')
    ax.set_xticklabels(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45, ha='right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

continent: Asia: 66.2% | Europe: 14.6% | North America: 12.9% | South America: 3.3% | Africa: 2.2% | Oceania: 0.8%
education_of_employee: Bachelor's: 40.2% | Master's: 37.8% | High School: 13.4% | Doctorate: 8.6%
has_job_experience: Y: 58.1% | N: 41.9%
requires_job_training: N: 88.4% | Y: 11.6%
region_of_employment: Northeast: 28.2% | South: 27.5% | West: 25.8% | Midwest: 16.9% | Island: 1.5%
unit_of_wage: Year: 90.1% | Hour: 8.5% | Week: 1.1% | Month: 0.3%
full_time_position: Y: 89.4% | N: 10.6%
case_status: Certified: 66.8% | Denied: 33.2%

Observations :

  • Most visa applicants are from Asia (66.2%), followed by Europe (14.6%) and North America (12.9%), indicating a strong representation from Asia in U.S. visa applications

  • A large proportion of employees have either a Bachelor’s (40.2%) or Master’s degree (37.8%), suggesting that most applicants possess higher education levels (while Doctorate (8.6%) and High School (13.4%) are less common)

  • A large proportion of applicants have job experience (58.1%), though a significant portion (41.9%) still do not.

  • 88.4% of applicants do not require job training, indicating that most candidates are considered job-ready

  • The majority of employment is intended in the Northeast (28.2%), South (27.5%), and West (25.8%) regions of the U.S., implying concentrated regional demand for foreign workers.

  • The most common unit of wage is Year (90.1%), followed by Hour (8.5%), with Month and Week being rare. 90.1% of prevailing wages reported on a yearly basis suggests that most job offers are long-term roles rather than short contracts.

  • 89.4% of positions are full-time, reinforcing the idea that employers are sponsoring foreign workers primarily for full-time roles.

  • Case approval rate is 66.8% (Certified), while 33.2% are Denied, indicating that about two-thirds of the visa applications in this dataset are successful

Bivariate Analysis¶

Numerical variables correlation using Heatmap¶

In [715]:
num_features = ['no_of_employees', 'yr_of_estab', 'prevailing_wage']

# defining the size of the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 6))

# plotting the heatmap for correlation
sns.heatmap(
    data[num_features].corr(),annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral"
);
plt.show()

Observations:

  • linear correlations within numerical features (no_of_employees, yr_of_estab, prevailing_wage) is checked
  • The heatmap shows very weak correlations (all near 0), meaning
    • These features are largely independent of one another.
    • There's no multicollinearity concern among them.
  • This doesn’t help much with bivariate analysis with respect to the target case_status

Numerical variables correlation with case status using Boxplot¶

In [716]:
# define the plot figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 12))

# plotting the boxplots for each numerical feature
for i, feature in enumerate(num_features):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i+1)    # assign a subplot in the main plot
    plt.title(f"({feature}) vs (case_status)")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x='case_status', y=feature)    # plot the boxplot

plt.tight_layout();   # to add spacing between plots
plt.show()

Observations:

  1. Number of Employees vs Case Status
    • Distribution is very similar across both outcomes with a large number of outliers.
    • Number of employees doesn't show strong discriminatory power visually.
  2. Year of Establishment vs Case Status
    • Slight trend that more recently established companies get slightly more approvals. But this difference is minimal.
  3. Prevailing Wage vs Case Status
    • Distributions of prevailing_wage for both Certified and Denied cases have marginal difference in the median
    • Both classes have:
      • Comparable medians.
      • Large overlaps in IQR (interquartile range).
      • Similar spread of outliers.

Categorical features and target variables using Crosstab¶

In [717]:
# define list of categorical features to plot countplot for analysis
cat_features = [
    'continent', 'education_of_employee', 'has_job_experience',
    'requires_job_training', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage',
    'full_time_position']

plt.figure(figsize=(12,9))

# plotting the boxplots for each numerical feature with the percentage in the bars
for i, feature in enumerate(cat_features):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i+1)    # assign a subplot in the main plot

    # creating a crosstab for case_status vs Category Feature
    tab = pd.crosstab(
        data[feature],
        data['case_status'],
        normalize='index'    # normalizing by dividing each row by its row total
    )

    # Plot within current subplot (ax passed explicitly),
    # creating a stacked bar chart from the normalized crosstab
    tab.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=plt.gca())

    #chart title
    plt.title(f'{feature} vs case_status')

    # x label
    plt.xlabel(feature)

    # y label
    plt.ylabel('Proportion')

    # adding a legend for the 'case_status' column
    plt.legend(title='case_status', loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1));

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations

  • Continent: Certification rates are fairly high across all continents, but Europe has the highest followed by Africa, Asia, North America and Oceania
  • Education : Doctorate and Master’s degree holders are more likely to be Certified compared to Bachelor's and High School levels.
  • Job Experience : Applicants with job experience (Y) show higher certification rates compared to those without experience (N), though the difference is moderate.
  • Requires Job Training : Both categories (requiring and not requiring job training) display very similar certification-to-denial ratios, suggesting this factor has little influence on outcomes.
  • Region of employment : MidWest region shows the highest certification rate, followed by South, Northeast, West and Island region having the lowest approval rate.
  • Unit of wage : Year-based wage positions show the highest certification rates (around 75%), followed by Month and Week, while Hour-based positions have the lowest success rates (around 35-40%).
  • Full time v Non full time : Both full-time (Y) and non-full-time (N) positions demonstrate nearly identical certification-to-denial ratios, indicating employment type has minimal impact.

Summary

  • Education level and wage unit structure emerge as the most discriminating factors, while job training requirements and full-time status appear to have no practical impact on visa certification outcomes.

Categorical features and target variables using Heatmap¶

In [718]:
# define categorical features
cat_features = [
    'continent', 'education_of_employee', 'has_job_experience',
    'requires_job_training', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage',
    'full_time_position'
]

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 9))

#iterate through each categorical feature and compare with outcome param case_status
for i, feature in enumerate(cat_features):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)

    # Crosstab of feature vs. case_status normalized by index (rows)
    tab = pd.crosstab(
        data[feature],
        data['case_status'],
        normalize='index'
    )

    # Heatmap of proportions
    sns.heatmap(
        tab,
        annot=True,
        fmt=".2f",
        cmap="YlGnBu",
        cbar=False
    )

    plt.title(f'{feature} vs case_status')
    plt.xlabel('case_status')
    plt.ylabel(feature)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations

  • Heatmap confirms the bivariate analysis observations made on categorical variables with outcome variable (case_status) from crosstab charts

2. Data Pre-processing¶

Missing value detection and treatment¶

In [719]:
# check for missing values
data.isnull().sum()
Out[719]:
continent                0
education_of_employee    0
has_job_experience       0
requires_job_training    0
no_of_employees          0
yr_of_estab              0
region_of_employment     0
prevailing_wage          0
unit_of_wage             0
full_time_position       0
case_status              0
dtype: int64
  • There are no missing values in the data

Duplicate value detection and treatment¶

In [720]:
# checking for duplicate values
data.duplicated().sum()
Out[720]:
np.int64(0)
  • There are no duplicate values in the data

Outlier Detection and treatment¶

In [721]:
# outlier detection using boxplot
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

for i, variable in enumerate(num_features):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, i + 1)
    plt.boxplot(data[variable], whis=1.5)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.title(variable)

plt.show()

Observations:

  1. no_of_employees:

    • Shows a very large number of extreme outliers on the upper end.
    • A few values go well beyond 600,000, which is not realistic for most companies.
    • Also, there are negative values which should be treated.

  2. yr_of_estab:

    • Contains many outliers on the lower end (years far before 1900).
    • Values before 1900 are to be treated

  3. prevailing_wage:

    • Shows a highly skewed distribution with extreme upper outliers, some > $300,000.
    • These are not necessarily wrong and can be treated for modeling.
    • Since this field is tied to unit_of_wage, wages should be normalized to a common scale (e.g., annualized) before analysis.

Treat no_of_employees outliers (negative values)¶

In [722]:
# get the count of no_of_employees less than zero
data[data['no_of_employees'] < 0].shape[0]
Out[722]:
33
  • There are 33 records with negative value. Given the small set of data, analysing them to check if they should be dropped or imputed can be done
In [723]:
data[data['no_of_employees'] < 0]
Out[723]:
continent education_of_employee has_job_experience requires_job_training no_of_employees yr_of_estab region_of_employment prevailing_wage unit_of_wage full_time_position case_status
245 Europe Master's N N -25 1980 Northeast 39452.9900 Year Y Certified
378 Asia Bachelor's N Y -11 2011 Northeast 32506.1400 Year Y Denied
832 South America Master's Y N -17 2002 South 129701.9400 Year Y Certified
2918 Asia Master's Y N -26 2005 Midwest 112799.4600 Year Y Certified
6439 Asia Bachelor's N N -14 2013 South 103.9700 Hour Y Denied
6634 Asia Bachelor's Y N -26 1923 West 5247.3200 Year Y Denied
7224 Europe Doctorate N N -25 1998 Midwest 141435.9500 Year Y Certified
7281 Asia High School N N -14 2000 Midwest 58488.5000 Year Y Denied
7318 Asia Bachelor's Y Y -26 2006 South 115005.6100 Year Y Certified
7761 Asia Master's N N -11 2009 Midwest 38457.5100 Year Y Certified
9872 Europe Master's Y N -26 1996 South 37397.0500 Year Y Certified
11493 Asia High School Y N -14 1999 South 27599.3500 Year Y Denied
13471 North America Master's N N -17 2003 Northeast 257.2413 Hour Y Denied
14022 Asia Bachelor's N Y -11 1946 Northeast 108403.5600 Year Y Certified
14146 Asia Bachelor's N Y -26 1954 West 81982.2700 Year Y Certified
14726 Asia Master's N N -11 2000 Midwest 167851.8000 Year Y Certified
15600 Asia Bachelor's N N -14 2014 South 24641.6100 Year Y Denied
15859 Asia High School N N -11 1969 South 44640.6000 Year Y Denied
16157 Asia Master's Y N -11 1994 South 62681.2500 Year Y Certified
16883 North America Bachelor's Y N -26 1968 Northeast 168.1558 Hour Y Denied
17006 Asia Doctorate Y N -11 1984 West 25753.5100 Year Y Denied
17655 North America Bachelor's Y N -17 2007 Northeast 129753.1800 Year Y Denied
17844 Asia Bachelor's N N -14 2012 West 29325.8500 Year Y Denied
17983 Asia Bachelor's N N -26 2004 South 84359.9800 Year Y Denied
20815 Asia Bachelor's N Y -17 1990 West 91897.5700 Year Y Certified
20984 Europe Doctorate Y N -14 1989 Midwest 37012.8000 Year Y Certified
21255 North America High School N N -25 1987 South 99405.4700 Year N Denied
21760 Asia Bachelor's Y N -25 2000 West 100463.5800 Year Y Certified
21944 Africa Master's Y N -25 1977 Midwest 79150.5100 Year Y Certified
22084 North America Bachelor's Y N -14 1980 West 691.0609 Hour Y Denied
22388 Asia Master's Y N -14 1986 South 17893.1100 Year Y Certified
23186 Asia Master's N Y -11 2007 Midwest 120195.3500 Year Y Certified
23476 Europe Master's Y N -11 2000 West 95072.7500 Year Y Denied

  • Negative value for the no_of_employees appears a data entry error and not an invalid data as all the applicant related information appears well distributed and relevant.

  • Employee count can be treated using median. Imputing with median will be done to the data after splitting to test and train as median is not influenced by extremely large values of employee count (that exist here)

  • It has better central tendency for skewed or dispersed data like employee counts

Treat yr_of_estab outliers¶

  • Year of establishment of the companies has a few values that are clearly invalid or unrealistic as the values range from 1800
  • Dropping the values from a base year e.g., 1900 might lead to loss of data. Hence, Age of the company will be computed w.r.to the current year for modeling using the simple computation company_age = current_year(2025) - year_of_establishment
  • Age provides numerical continuity and a standardized scale that models can lern better from compared to large values like 1800/1900
In [724]:
# create 'company_age' by subtracting year of establishment from current year (2025)
data['company_age'] = 2025 - data['yr_of_estab']

# drop the original 'yr_of_estab' column as it's no longer needed after deriving 'company_age'
data.drop(columns='yr_of_estab', inplace=True)

Treat prevailing_wage outliers¶

  • prevailing_wage relates to unit_of_wage categorical column. EDA showed that visa approval rates vary by unit_of_wage and less impacted by prevailing_wage

  • prevailing_wage has a high dispersion due to this i.e., mean ≈ 74k, std ≈ 52k and ranges from \$2.13 to \$319210. Analysing with inconsistent prevailing_wage could impact model performance i.e., a \$300 weekly wage isn't comparable to a \$300 yearly wage.

  • The prevailing_wage will not be included in the analysis

In [725]:
# describe the prevailing wage data distribution
data['prevailing_wage'].describe().T
Out[725]:
count     25480.000000
mean      74455.814592
std       52815.942327
min           2.136700
25%       34015.480000
50%       70308.210000
75%      107735.512500
max      319210.270000
Name: prevailing_wage, dtype: float64
In [726]:
# group prevailing wage by unit of wage to understand the data distribution
data.groupby('unit_of_wage')['prevailing_wage'].describe().sort_values(by='max', ascending=False)
Out[726]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
unit_of_wage
Year 22962.0 81228.077133 49951.473223 100.0000 43715.9550 76174.5000 111341.9600 319210.2700
Week 272.0 85606.820515 44802.704810 2183.2300 51408.2775 85075.8200 111331.9100 280175.9500
Month 89.0 87592.864045 59525.124924 1599.2800 44986.2400 81826.0100 121629.6000 264362.9500

In [727]:
# draw a boxplot to understand the wage distribution by unit of wage
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='unit_of_wage', y='prevailing_wage')
plt.title('Boxplot of Prevailing Wage by Unit of Wage')
plt.xlabel('Unit of Wage')
plt.ylabel('Prevailing Wage')
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Yearly Wages:

    • High spread with many outliers above ~$200,000.
    • Median wage appears lower than that of Month and Week units.

  • Monthly & Weekly Wages:

    • Higher median wages than yearly (likely due to smaller sample sizes).
    • Fewer but still notable outliers, reaching ~$250,000.

  • Hourly Wages:

    • Lowest overall, among wages and the distributon is compact
    • There are nearly no outliers, reflecting lower compensation levels

Considerations

  • Given the challenges in the data for prevailing_wage across different units, normalising using basic techniques like 8hrs per day or 5 day working weeks for 52 weeks etc., the wages could lead to even more impact of outliers in the data

Conclusion

  • The prevailing_wage will not be included in the analysis

Feature engineering¶

Label encode categorical columns (binary/categorical target columns)¶

  • Following columns will be label encoded to 0 or 1
    1. as_job_experience
    2. requires_job_training
    3. full_time_position
    4. case_status
In [728]:
# columns for label encoding
label_cols = ['has_job_experience', 'requires_job_training', 'full_time_position', 'case_status']

#loop through each column and using fit_transform normalise the labels
for col in label_cols:
    le = LabelEncoder()
    data[col] = le.fit_transform(data[col])
In [729]:
#verify label encoding
data.head()

One-hot encode categorical columns (nominal)¶

  • Following columns will be one hot encoded
    1. continent
    2. education_of_employee
    3. region_of_employment
    4. unit_of_wage
In [730]:
#one hot encode columns
onehot_cols = ['continent', 'education_of_employee', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage']

#one hot encode and drop_first to avoid dummy variable trap
data = pd.get_dummies(data, columns=onehot_cols, drop_first=True)

Prepare data for modeling¶

Split data to train, validation, test sets¶

In [733]:
# Drop 'prevailing_wage' from features and target variable 'case_status'
X = data.drop(['case_status', 'prevailing_wage'], axis=1)

# Set target variable
y = data['case_status']
In [734]:
# Data split into 2 parts : temporary and test
X_temp, X_test, y_temp, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

# Temporary set split into train and validation
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_temp, y_temp, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y_temp
)

# Create table of dataset shapes with y having 1 col
shapes = pd.DataFrame({
    'Dataset': ['X_train', 'y_train', 'X_val', 'y_val', 'X_test','y_test'],
    'Rows':   [X_train.shape[0], y_train.shape[0],
               X_val.shape[0], y_val.shape[0],
               X_test.shape[0], y_test.shape[0]],
    'Columns': [X_train.shape[1], 1,
                X_val.shape[1], 1,
                X_test.shape[1], 1]
})

print(shapes)

   Dataset   Rows  Columns
0  X_train  16307       20
1  y_train  16307        1
2    X_val   4077       20
3    y_val   4077        1
4   X_test   5096       20
5   y_test   5096        1

Verify class balance¶

In [735]:
# Create table class balance for whole data, train set, validation set, and test set
class_balance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Dataset' : ['y', 'y_train', 'y_val', 'y_test'],
    '0' : [y.value_counts(1)[0], y_train.value_counts(1)[0], y_val.value_counts(1)[0], y_test.value_counts(1)[0]],
    '1' : [y.value_counts(1)[1], y_train.value_counts(1)[1], y_val.value_counts(1)[1], y_test.value_counts(1)[1]]
})

print(class_balance)

   Dataset         0         1
0        y  0.667896  0.332104
1  y_train  0.667873  0.332127
2    y_val  0.667893  0.332107
3   y_test  0.667975  0.332025
  • The data is stratified across train, validation and test sets as it is in the original data set with 66.8% of 0 i.e., Denied and 33.3% of 1 Certified

Impute the no_of_employees¶

  • The no_of_employees will be imputed using mean across training, validation and testing data sets
In [736]:
# Replace negative values with np.nan
X_train.loc[X_train["no_of_employees"] < 0, "no_of_employees"] = np.nan
X_val.loc[X_val["no_of_employees"] < 0, "no_of_employees"] = np.nan
X_test.loc[X_test["no_of_employees"] < 0, "no_of_employees"] = np.nan

# Impute the missing values with SimpleImputer
imp_median = SimpleImputer(missing_values=np.nan, strategy="median")

# fit the imputer on train data and transform the train data
X_train["no_of_employees"] = imp_median.fit_transform(X_train[["no_of_employees"]])

# transform the validation and test data using the imputer fit on train data
X_val["no_of_employees"] = imp_median.transform(X_val[["no_of_employees"]])
X_test["no_of_employees"] = imp_median.transform(X_test[["no_of_employees"]])
In [737]:
# validate the count of no_of_employees less than zero to be none
print(X_train[X_train['no_of_employees'] < 0].shape[0])
print(X_val[X_val['no_of_employees'] < 0].shape[0])
print(X_test[X_test['no_of_employees'] < 0].shape[0])

0
0
0

3. Model Building - Original Data¶

Model Evaluation Criterion¶

What does OFLC want?¶

  • A reliable classification model to predict VISA approval outcomes.

  • Insights into key decision factors that influence approvals/denials.

Reference from problem statement : Recommend a suitable profile for the applicants for whom the visa should be certified or denied based on the drivers that significantly influence the case status.

Where can the model go wrong?¶

  • False Positives : Predicting a visa will be approved when it gets denied in reality could mislead employers and waste resources.

  • False Negatives : Predicting a visa will be denied when it gets approved in reality may unfairly reject strong candidates.

Which case is more important?¶

False Positives are more important to minimize, as predicting visa approval for candidates who ultimately get denied can:

  • Mislead employers into investing time and resources in unqualified candidates.
  • Undermine the credibility and efficiency of the visa processing system.
  • This could also lead to challenges like non-compliance with regulations.

False Negatives are tolerable (but worth monitoring) as EasyVisa could reject someone who could have been approved

Which metric to optimize?¶

Metrics to optimise in order

  1. Precision : Focuses on minimizing False Positives. High precision = OFLC reviews mostly truly certifiable cases.

  2. F1-Score : Balances precision and recall; useful to ensure reasonable overall performance.

  3. Recall : Important, but secondary — we can afford to miss a few good cases if it helps reduce OFLC’s load.

How to reduce the losses?¶

  • Use robust feature engineering and ensemble models (e.g., AdaBoost, GradientBoost, XGBoost, Bagging, Random Forest).
  • Apply class imbalance handling (SMOTE, Random Under Sampling, class weights).
  • Use hyper parameter tuning techniques

Utility functions¶

In [738]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn
def model_performance_classification(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [739]:
# function to create a single DataFrame of training, validation performances
def model_performances(train, validation):

  #combine the dataframes across rows
  combined = pd.concat([train, validation],
                     axis=0,
                     keys=["Training", "Validation"])
  # drop the index
  combined.reset_index(level=1, drop=True)
  return combined
In [1092]:
# function to plot the confusion matrix of training and validation, side by side
def plot_confusion_matrices_v2(model, training_predictors, training_target,
                               validation_predictors, validation_target,
                               validation_label = 'Validation Confusion Matrix'):
    """
    Plots side-by-side confusion matrices for training and validation datasets.

    model: trained classifier
    training_predictors, training_target: training features and labels
    validation_predictors, validation_target: validation features and labels
    """

    def compute_cm_labels(cm):
        labels = np.asarray(
            [
                f"{item}\n{item/cm.sum():.2%}"
                for item in cm.flatten()
            ]
        ).reshape(2, 2)
        return labels

    # Predict on training and validation data
    y_train_pred = model.predict(training_predictors)
    y_val_pred = model.predict(validation_predictors)

    # Compute confusion matrices
    cm_train = confusion_matrix(training_target, y_train_pred)
    cm_val = confusion_matrix(validation_target, y_val_pred)

    # Compute labels
    labels_train = compute_cm_labels(cm_train)
    labels_val = compute_cm_labels(cm_val)

    # Plot side-by-side
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10,4))

    # create training confusion matrix
    sns.heatmap(cm_train, annot=labels_train, fmt="", ax=axes[0])
    axes[0].set_title("Training Confusion Matrix")
    axes[0].set_xlabel("Predicted label")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("True label")

    # create testing / validation confusion matrix
    sns.heatmap(cm_val, annot=labels_val, fmt="", ax=axes[1])
    axes[1].set_title(validation_label)
    axes[1].set_xlabel("Predicted label")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("True label")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [741]:
# function to create the dataframe comparing original and sampled data
def create_sample_comparison(predictor, validator, predictor_sampled, validator_sampled):
  sampled_data_frame = {
    'Label 1 Count': [
        sum(validator == 1),
        sum(validator_sampled == 1)
    ],
    'Label 0 Count': [
        sum(validator == 0),
        sum(validator_sampled == 0)
    ],
    'X Shape': [
        predictor.shape,
        predictor_sampled.shape
    ],
    'y Shape': [
        validator.shape,
        validator_sampled.shape
    ]
  }
  # Create the DataFrame with appropriate index labels
  df_summary = pd.DataFrame(sampled_data_frame, index=['Before Sampling', 'After Sampling'])
  return df_summary

M1 : Decision Tree (sklearn default)¶

  • A basic DecisionTree model will be built.
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [742]:
# creating an instance of the decision tree model
# random_state sets a seed value and enables reproducibility
dtree_default = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the training data
dtree_default.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[742]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In [743]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(dtree_default, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [811]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
dtree_default_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    dtree_default, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
dtree_default_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    dtree_default, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
dtree_performance = model_performances(dtree_default_train_perf, dtree_default_val_perf)
dtree_performance
Out[811]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.999877 0.999631 1.000000 0.999815
Validation 0 0.660780 0.488922 0.489283 0.489102

Model Summary¶

  • Training F1 score is excellent at 0.999, indicating near-perfect performance.

  • Validation F1 score drops sharply to 0.489, showing severe overfitting.

  • Precision on training is perfect (1.0), but falls to 0.489 on validation.

  • Large gap between training and validation metrics suggests poor generalization.

  • Model is likely memorizing training data rather than learning general patterns.

M2 : Bagging Classifier¶

  • A basic Bagging Classifier model will be built. Base estimator for bagging classifier is a decision tree by default
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [745]:
#create bagging classifier and fit to training data
bagging_classifier=BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)
bagging_classifier.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[745]:
BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)
In [746]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(bagging_classifier, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [821]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
bagging_classifier_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    bagging_classifier, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
bagging_classifier_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    bagging_classifier, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
bagging_classifier_performance = model_performances(bagging_classifier_train_perf, bagging_classifier_val_perf)
bagging_classifier_performance
Out[821]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.976452 0.936669 0.991983 0.963533
Validation 0 0.707873 0.460857 0.575115 0.511685

Model summary¶

  • Training F1: 0.963 — still very high, indicating near-perfect learning on training data.
  • Validation F1: 0.511 — much lower, showing the model struggles on unseen data.
  • F1 gap: A large drop, confirming overfitting is present.
  • Precision gap: 0.991 (train) vs. 0.575 (val) is also significant, reinforcing overfitting.
  • Compared to Decision Tree: Smaller performance gap, but still fails to generalize well.

M3 : Random Forest Classifier¶

  • A basic RandomForestClassifier model will be built.
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [748]:
#Train the random forest classifier
rf_classifier=RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)
rf_classifier.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[748]:

In [749]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(rf_classifier, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [825]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
rf_classifier_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    rf_classifier, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
rf_classifier_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    rf_classifier, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
rf_classifier_performance = model_performances(rf_classifier_train_perf, rf_classifier_val_perf)
rf_classifier_performance
Out[825]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.999755 0.999446 0.999815 0.999631
Validation 0 0.708119 0.491137 0.570326 0.527778

Model summary¶

  • Training F1 score is 0.999, indicating near-perfect fit on training data.
  • Validation F1 score is 0.527, showing moderate generalization with a large performance gap.
  • Validation precision is 0.570, suggesting reasonable reliability in positive predictions.
  • Significant difference between training and validation F1 confirms overfitting.
  • Compared to BaggingClassifier, RandomForest performs slightly better on validation, but still suffers from overfitting.

M4 : AdaBoost Classifier¶

  • A basic AdBoostClassifier model will be built.
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [751]:
#Train the adaboost classifier
ada_boost_classifier = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
ada_boost_classifier.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[751]:
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
In [752]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(ada_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [826]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
ada_boost_classifier_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
ada_boost_classifier_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
ada_boost_classifier_performance = model_performances(ada_boost_classifier_train_perf, ada_boost_classifier_val_perf)
ada_boost_classifier_performance
Out[826]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.735267 0.427068 0.655798 0.517276
Validation 0 0.734118 0.447563 0.643312 0.527875

Model summary¶

  • Training F1 score is 0.517, indicating moderate learning on the training data.

  • Validation F1 score is 0.527, very close to training, suggesting minimal overfitting. But also limited overall performance

  • Validation precision is 0.643, the highest among all models so far, indicating strong reliability in positive predictions.

  • Low recall (0.447) on validation indicates many false negatives

  • Consistency between training and validation metrics reflects better generalization. May require better feature engineering or hyperparameter tuning.

M5 : Gradient Boost Classifier¶

  • A basic GradientBoostClassifier model will be built.
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [754]:
#Train the gradient classifier
gradient_boost_classifier = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)
gradient_boost_classifier.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[754]:
GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.

In [755]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(gradient_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [827]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
gradient_boost_classifier_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    gradient_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
gradient_boost_classifier_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    gradient_boost_classifier, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
gradient_boost_classifier_performance = model_performances(gradient_boost_classifier_train_perf, gradient_boost_classifier_val_perf)
gradient_boost_classifier_performance
Out[827]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.753112 0.502770 0.671351 0.574958
Validation 0 0.747854 0.512555 0.653484 0.574503

Model summary¶

  • F1 scores for training (0.574) and validation (0.574) are nearly identical, indicating good generalization.
  • Precision is 0.653, making the model reliable in positive predictions.
  • Recall is moderate (0.512), suggesting the model misses a significant portion of true positives.
  • Overall performance is balanced and better than previous models, with lesser overfitting. Further improvement (better feature engineering or hyperparameter tuning) is needed in recall and overall accuracy (0.747) to enhance effectiveness.

M6 : XG Boost Classifier¶

  • A basic XG Boost Classifier model will be built.
  • The model will be fit on training data set and predicted on validation data set
  • All the 4 metrics F1 Score, Precision, Recall and Accuracy will be studied with priority to F1 score

Note : No hyper parameters will be provided.

In [757]:
# Train the XG Boost classifier.
xg_boost_classifier = XGBClassifier(random_state=1, eval_metric = 'logloss')
xg_boost_classifier.fit(X_train,y_train)
Out[757]:
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=None, colsample_bynode=None,
              colsample_bytree=None, device=None, early_stopping_rounds=None,
              enable_categorical=False, eval_metric='logloss',
              feature_types=None, feature_weights=None, gamma=None,
              grow_policy=None, importance_type=None,
              interaction_constraints=None, learning_rate=None, max_bin=None,
              max_cat_threshold=None, max_cat_to_onehot=None,

              min_child_weight=None, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=None, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)

In [758]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(xg_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val)
In [828]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
xg_boost_classifier_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier, X_train, y_train
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
xg_boost_classifier_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
xg_boost_classifier_performance = model_performances(xg_boost_classifier_train_perf, xg_boost_classifier_val_perf)
xg_boost_classifier_performance
Out[828]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.825596 0.632201 0.800748 0.706562
Validation 0 0.736326 0.508863 0.626934 0.561761

Model summary¶

  • Training F1 score is 0.706, showing strong learning on the training data.
  • Validation F1 score drops to 0.561, indicating some overfitting.
  • Validation precision is 0.620, suggesting decent reliability in positive predictions.
  • Recall falls from 0.632 (train) to 0.508 (val), meaning the model misses many true positives.
  • Overall performance is solid, but the gap between training and validation shows room for better generalization.

4. Model Building - Oversampled Data¶

  • Training Data will be oversampled for the above models using SMOTE
  • The model trained on oversampled data will be validated against the validation dataset

Oversample data using SMOTE¶

In [760]:
# Fit SMOTE on train data(Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique)
# Sample the minority 'Certified' class to 0.67
smote = SMOTE(sampling_strategy=0.67, k_neighbors=5, random_state=1)
X_train_over, y_train_over = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
In [761]:
# get the new dataset size before and after over sampling
create_sample_comparison(X_train, y_train, X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[761]:
Label 1 Count Label 0 Count X Shape y Shape
Before Sampling 5416 10891 (16307, 20) (16307,)
After Sampling 7296 10891 (18187, 20) (18187,)

Observations

  • We can notice that the minority class samples i.e, 1 (visa Certified) has increased from 5416 to 7296 as per the sampling_strategy of 2/3rd or 0.67

M1 : Decision Tree oversampled (sklearn default)¶

  • The model will be trained on oversampled data
In [762]:
#Train the decision tree on oversampled data
dtree_over = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the over sampled training data
dtree_over.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[762]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)
In [763]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(dtree_over, X_train_over, y_train_over, X_val, y_val)
In [816]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
dtree_over_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    dtree_over, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
dtree_over_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    dtree_over, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
dtree_over_performance = model_performances(dtree_over_train_perf, dtree_over_val_perf)
dtree_over_performance
Out[816]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.999890 0.999726 1.0 0.999863
Validation 0 0.667893 0.522895 0.5 0.511191

Model Summary¶

  • Oversampled Decision tree model is performing marginally better on F1 Score, precision, Recall compared to the origin model.
  • Still the model is overfitting as training scores are near perfect, but the validation scores are dropping significantly.

M2 : Bagging Classifier oversampled¶

In [765]:
#Train the bagging classifier on oversampled data
bagging_classifier_over = BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the over sampled training data
bagging_classifier_over.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[765]:

    bagging_classifier_over, X_train_over, y_train_over

Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.977621 0.951754 0.992142 0.971529
Validation 0 0.701987 0.473412 0.560805 0.513416

Model Summary¶

  • There is a marginal improvement in F1 score after oversampling compared to the model built on the original data, but the gain is not significant.
  • The model still shows signs of overfitting, with a noticeable gap between training and validation metrics such as F1, Precision, and Recall.

  • The model will be trained on oversampled data

Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.999890 1.000000 0.999726 0.999863
Validation 0 0.709836 0.514771 0.569910 0.540939

Model Summary¶

  • Slight improvement in validation F1 score, accuracy after oversampling and also recall has a modest gain.
  • Training metrics are near-perfect in both cases, suggesting the model is highly overfitting regardless of oversampling.

M4 : AdaBoostClassifier oversampled¶

  • The model will be trained on oversampled data
In [771]:

# fitting the model to the over sampled training data

AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
In [772]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(ada_boost_classifier_over, X_train_over, y_train_over, X_val, y_val)
In [824]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
ada_boost_classifier_over_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier_over, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
ada_boost_classifier_over_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier_over, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
ada_boost_classifier_over_performance = model_performances(ada_boost_classifier_over_train_perf, ada_boost_classifier_over_val_perf)
ada_boost_classifier_over_performance
Out[824]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.740034 0.538240 0.742906 0.624225
Validation 0 0.734609 0.469719 0.636000 0.540357

Model Summary¶

  • F1 score and recall have improved marginally after oversampling, compared to the original data model. But the values of recall and F1 are relatively low, indicating the model is missing many positive (minority) cases.
  • Precision scores are reasonable but not strong. This model may be a baseline, but would likely need improvement via tuning, better features, or alternative algorithms for production use.
  • The model doesn't appear to be overfitting and is generalising, as the gaps are modest, not extreme between training and validation data of the oversampled model

M5 : Gradient Boost Classifier oversampled¶

  • The model will be trained on oversampled data
In [774]:
#Train the gradient boost classifier on oversampled data
gradient_boost_classifier_over = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the over sampled training data
gradient_boost_classifier_over.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

Out[830]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.769396 0.636513 0.750727 0.688919
Validation 0 0.745156 0.539143 0.637555 0.584234

Model summary¶

  • Oversampling has improved all training metrics, for Recall, F1 showing better learning of the minority class.

  • Validation F1 also improved slightly (to 0.584), mainly due to higher recall, suggesting improved generalization.

  • Small gap between training and validation metrics indicates limited overfitting and a good balance between learning and generalization.

  • The main challenges remain around limited generalization, moderate recall, and mild overfitting

M6 : XG Boost Classifier oversampled¶

  • The model will be trained on oversampled data

    xg_boost_classifier_over, X_train_over, y_train_over

Out[831]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.828284 0.707374 0.839323 0.767720
Validation 0 0.730684 0.508863 0.614082 0.556543

Model Summary¶

  • Training-validation gap widened slightly in F1 (0.76 vs. 0.55), suggesting mild overfitting post-oversampling.

  • Validation recall and F1, while improved, are still moderate, leaving room for boosting generalization further.

  • Precision dipped slightly on validation after oversampling, which may be a trade-off for higher recall.

5. Model Building - Undersampled Data¶

Undersample data using SMOTE¶

In [780]:
# fit random under sampler on the train data
random_under_sampler = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=1, sampling_strategy = 1)
X_train_under, y_train_under = random_under_sampler.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
In [781]:
# get the new dataset size before and after under sampling
create_sample_comparison(X_train, y_train, X_train_under, y_train_under)
Out[781]:
Label 1 Count Label 0 Count X Shape y Shape
Before Sampling 5416 10891 (16307, 20) (16307,)
After Sampling 5416 5416 (10832, 20) (10832,)

M1 : Decision Tree undersampled (sklearn default)¶

  • The model will be trained on under sampled data
In [784]:
#Train the decision tree on under sampled data
dtree_under = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the under sampled training data
dtree_under.fit(X_train_under, y_train_under)
Out[784]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)

# compute performance metrics on training data set
dtree_under_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    dtree_under, X_train_under, y_train_under
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data

dtree_under_performance
Out[832]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 1.000000 1.000000 1.000000 1.000000
Validation 0 0.628403 0.636632 0.457294 0.532263

Model summary¶

  • F1 score and recall have marginal improvement for the undersampled model compared to the decision tree models from original data and oversampled data. Also, accuracy and precision have dropped in the undersampled model

  • The model is still overfitting as the difference in training validation data is high

M2 : Bagging Classifier undersampled¶

  • The model will be trained on under sampled data
In [787]:

bagging_classifier_under.fit(X_train_under, y_train_under)

    bagging_classifier_under, X_train_under, y_train_under

Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.979321 0.967873 0.990552 0.979081
Validation 0 0.677704 0.638109 0.511848 0.568047

Model summary¶

  • Similar pattern like undersampled decision tree can be observed here in undersampled bagging classifier. There is a noticeable increase in recall and marginal increase in F1 score. But, there is also a drop in precision and accuracy

  • Undersampled bagging classifier model is still overfitting as the gap is high between oversamples training and validation data

M3: Random Forest Classifier undersampled¶

  • The model will be trained on under sampled data
In [791]:
#Train the random forest classifier on under sampled data
rf_classifier_under = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)

# fitting the model to the under sampled training data
rf_classifier_under.fit(X_train_under, y_train_under)

In [834]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
rf_classifier_under_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    rf_classifier_under, X_train_under, y_train_under
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
rf_classifier_under_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    rf_classifier_under, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
rf_classifier_under_performance = model_performances(rf_classifier_under_train_perf, rf_classifier_under_val_perf)
rf_classifier_under_performance

Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.999908 0.999815 1.000000 0.999908
Validation 0 0.662252 0.682422 0.493854 0.573023

Model summary¶

  • F1 score has marginally increased and recall has significantly increased due to the under sampling, but accuracy and precision have dropped
  • Undersampled random forest classifier model is still overfitting as the gap is high between oversamples training and validation data

  • The model will be trained on under sampled data

Model Summary¶

Model summary¶

  • For undersampled XGBoost, precision and accuracy have dropped. Recall has increased and also F1 score marginally

  • The model is also moderately overfitting as there is gap between training and validation metrics

6. Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Top 3 best performing models from the total 18 models (6 x3) will be chosen. i.e., from 6 different techniques x 3 different models per technique

  1. Decision Tree
  2. Bagging
  3. Random Forest
  4. AdaBoost
  5. Gradient Boost
  6. XG Boost

Listing the model performances¶

In [844]:
# Create the matrix to display the techniques and the models with their performance on training and testing data
all_model_names = [
  'Decision Tree simple',
  'Decision Tree oversampled',
  'Decision Tree undersampled',
  'Bagging simple',
  'Bagging oversampled',
  'Bagging undersampled',
  'Random Forest simple',
  'Random Forest oversampled',
  'Random Forest undersampled',
  'Ada Boost simple',
  'Ada Boost oversampled',
  'Ada Boost undersampled',
  'Gradient Boost simple',
  'Gradient Boost oversampled',
  'Gradient Boost undersampled',
  'XG Boost simple',
  'XG Boost oversampled',
  'XG Boost undersampled'
]

all_models_without_tuning = pd.concat([
	dtree_performance,
	dtree_over_performance,
	dtree_under_performance,
	bagging_classifier_performance,
	bagging_classifier_over_performance,
	bagging_classifier_under_performance,
	rf_classifier_performance,
	rf_classifier_over_performance,
	rf_classifier_under_performance,
	ada_boost_classifier_performance,
	ada_boost_classifier_over_performance,
	ada_boost_classifier_under_performance,
	gradient_boost_classifier_performance,
	gradient_boost_classifier_over_performance,
	gradient_boost_classifier_under_performance,
	xg_boost_classifier_performance,
	xg_boost_classifier_over_performance,
	xg_boost_classifier_under_performance
  ], axis =0, keys = all_model_names)
all_models_without_tuning
Out[844]:

Validation 0 0.708119 0.491137 0.570326 0.527778

Validation 0 0.709836 0.514771 0.569910 0.540939
Random Forest undersampled Training 0 0.999908 0.999815 1.000000 0.999908
Validation 0 0.662252 0.682422 0.493854 0.573023
