Validation 0 0.734118 0.447563 0.643312 0.527875
Ada Boost oversampled Training 0 0.740034 0.538240 0.742906 0.624225
Validation 0 0.734609 0.469719 0.636000 0.540357
Ada Boost undersampled Training 0 0.690547 0.672267 0.697777 0.684785
Validation 0 0.695364 0.685377 0.532110 0.599096
Gradient Boost simple Training 0 0.753112 0.502770 0.671351 0.574958
Validation 0 0.747854 0.512555 0.653484 0.574503
Gradient Boost oversampled Training 0 0.769396 0.636513 0.750727 0.688919
Validation 0 0.745156 0.539143 0.637555 0.584234
Gradient Boost undersampled Training 0 0.717688 0.696640 0.727255 0.711618
Validation 0 0.706156 0.694239 0.545244 0.610786
XG Boost simple Training 0 0.825596 0.632201 0.800748 0.706562
Validation 0 0.736326 0.508863 0.626934 0.561761
XG Boost oversampled Training 0 0.828284 0.707374 0.839323 0.767720
Validation 0 0.730684 0.508863 0.614082 0.556543
XG Boost undersampled Training 0 0.833272 0.827733 0.837005 0.832343
Validation 0 0.687761 0.703840 0.522192 0.599560

Selecting the Top '3' Models for hyper-parameter tuning¶

Selected Models (oversampled)¶

1. Gradient Boost (Oversampled)¶

  • Precision: 0.6376, one of the highest, aligning with the goal to reduce false positives.
  • F1 Score: 0.5842, strong balance between precision and recall.
  • Recall: 0.5391, decent enough to avoid high false negatives.
  • Handles class imbalance well with oversampling + boosting synergy.

2. XGBoost (Oversampled)¶

  • Precision: 0.6141, competitive and consistent across variations.
  • F1 Score: 0.5565, reliable balance of predictive strength.
  • Recall: 0.5089, well above average for high-precision models.
  • Robust and scalable, great for complex, high-volume production use cases.

3. AdaBoost (Oversampled)¶

  • Precision: 0.6360, very close to Gradient Boost. Also, great for minimizing incorrect visa certifications.
  • F1 Score: 0.5404, solid balance, outperforming simpler AdaBoost.
  • Recall: 0.4697, acceptable considering high precision.
  • Simpler model with fast training, suitable for early-stage deployments.

Why Undersampled Models Were Not Chosen¶

  • Despite higher recall (e.g., 0.70+), they generally show low precision (e.g., 0.52 for XGBoost undersampled), increasing false positives.

  • F1 scores, though reasonable, are lower than oversampled counterparts.

  • Undersampling discards valid data, possibly leading to weaker generalization and missed approval-worthy cases.

Why Simple Models Were Not Chosen¶

  • Simple models (e.g., Decision Tree, Bagging, AdaBoost without balancing) often show signs of overfitting: extremely high training scores but weaker validation precision.
  • Typically have lower F1 scores (e.g., 0.49–0.53 range), showing poor balance.
  • Struggle with class imbalance, especially without any sampling strategy.
  • Less robust to real-world variance and data drift.

1. Tuning AdaBoost (oversampled)¶

Hyper Parameters

Following hyper parameters will be used for AdaBoost tuning

  1. base_estimator: The base estimator from which the boosted ensemble is built. By default the base estimator is a decision tree with max_depth=1 2.n_estimators: The maximum number of estimators at which boosting is terminated. Default value is 50.
  2. learning_rate: Learning rate shrinks the contribution of each classifier by learning_rate. There is a trade-off between learning_rate and n_estimators.

Cross Validation

For cross validation GridSearchCV will be used

  • scoring will be done for precision and F1 score
  • To parallelise n_jobs=-1 will be used
In [1076]:
# Grid of parameters to choose from
ada_parameters = {
    #Different max_depth for estimator
    "estimator":[DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1),
                 DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=4, random_state=1),
                 DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": [100, 200, 300, 400],
    "learning_rate": [0.1, 0.2]
}

# Precision and F1 scoring used to compare parameter combinations
ada_scoring = {
    'precision' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.precision_score),
    'f1' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)
}

# Create and run the grid search for all above combinations of hyper params
# use ada_boost_classifier_over model
ada_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(ada_boost_classifier_over,
                        ada_parameters,
                        scoring=ada_scoring,
                        refit='f1',
                        cv=5,
                        n_jobs=-1)
ada_grid_obj = ada_grid_obj.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

# Set the clf to the best combination of parameters
ada_boost_classifier_tuned = ada_grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
ada_boost_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[1076]:
AdaBoostClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5,
                                                    random_state=1),
                   learning_rate=0.2, n_estimators=400, random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
AdaBoostClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5,
                                                    random_state=1),
                   learning_rate=0.2, n_estimators=400, random_state=1)
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1)
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1)
In [1077]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(ada_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over, X_val, y_val)
In [1078]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
ada_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
ada_boost_classifier_tuned_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    ada_boost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
ada_boost_classifier_tuned_performance = model_performances(ada_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf, ada_boost_classifier_tuned_val_perf)
ada_boost_classifier_tuned_performance
Out[1078]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.769286 0.642681 0.746894 0.69088
Validation 0 0.742458 0.546529 0.629252 0.58498

Model summary¶

  • model is overfitting very mild as the difference between oversampled training data and validation data is minimal
  • There is a minor drop in precision value in the tuned model compared to the non-tuned over sampled model
  • Improvement on F1 score and Recall is marginal

Importances¶

In [1079]:
# compute and print the important features
ada_boost_importances = ada_boost_classifier_tuned.feature_importances_
ada_indices = np.argsort(ada_boost_importances)
ada_feature_names = list(X.columns)

# plot the feature importance
plt.figure(figsize=(8,8))
plt.title('AdaBoost Feature Importances')
plt.barh(range(len(ada_indices)), ada_boost_importances[ada_indices], color='violet', align='center')
plt.yticks(range(len(ada_indices)), [ada_feature_names[i] for i in ada_indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.show()

Observations

  • Adaboost hyper-tuned model considers the applicant country Asia as the important feature for applicants getting visas certified
  • This is followed by various regions of employment and education level masters. High school education ranks high, but could be in negative filter context

2. Tuning Gradient Boost (oversampled)¶

Hyper Parameters

Following hyper parameters will be used for Gradient Boost tuning on the oversampled model

  1. n_estimators: number of boosting stages to perform. 2.subsample: fraction of samples to be used for fitting the individual base learners.
  2. max_features: number of features to consider when looking for the best split
  3. learning_rate : shrinks the contribution of each tree by learning_rate. There is a trade-off between learning_rate and n_estimators

Cross Validation

For cross validation GridSearchCV will be used

  • scoring will be done for precision and F1 score
  • To parallelise n_jobs=-1 will be used
In [1031]:
# Grid of hyper parameters to choose from for GridSearchCV using Gradient Boosting
gb_parameters = {
    "n_estimators": [100, 150, 200, 250],
    "subsample":[0.8,0.9,1],
    "max_features":[0.7,0.8,0.9,1],
    "learning_rate":[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]
}

# Precision and F1 scoring used to compare parameter combinations
gb_scoring = {
    'precision' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.precision_score),
    'f1' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)
}

# Create and run the grid search for all above combinations of hyper params
# use gradient_boost_classifier_over model
gb_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(gradient_boost_classifier_over,
                        gb_parameters,
                        scoring=gb_scoring,
                        refit='f1',
                        cv=5,
                        n_jobs=-1)
gb_grid_obj = gb_grid_obj.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

# Set the clf to the best combination of parameters
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned = gb_grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[1031]:
GradientBoostingClassifier(max_features=0.7, n_estimators=150, random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.9)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
GradientBoostingClassifier(max_features=0.7, n_estimators=150, random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.9)
In [1032]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(gradient_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over, X_val, y_val)
In [1033]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    gradient_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    gradient_boost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_performance = model_performances(gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf, gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_val_perf)
gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_performance
Out[1033]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.77165 0.634731 0.756823 0.690421
Validation 0 0.74859 0.538405 0.645704 0.587193

Model Summary¶

  • There is a marginal increase in Precision compared to the non-hyperparam tuned model
  • Other metrics are almost the same between the tuned-oversampled-model to the non-tuned-oversampled model
  • Model is overfitting very mild as the difference between oversampled training data and validation data is minimal

Importances¶

In [1034]:
# compute and print the important features
gradient_boost_importances = gradient_boost_classifier_tuned.feature_importances_
gb_indices = np.argsort(gradient_boost_importances)
gb_feature_names = list(X.columns)

# plot the feature importance
plt.figure(figsize=(8,8))
plt.title('Gradient Boost Feature Importances')
plt.barh(range(len(gb_indices)), gradient_boost_importances[gb_indices], color='violet', align='center')
plt.yticks(range(len(gb_indices)), [gb_feature_names[i] for i in gb_indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.show()

Observations

  • GradientBoost hyper parameter tuned model considers the employee high school as the important feature for applicants getting visas certified. This is followed by Job experience and Masters education

  • It can also be observed the features Job experience, Masters education, belonging to Asia, Yearly wage are at the top 6 for both both GradientBoost hyperparameter tuned model and AdaBoost hyperparameter tuned models. High School education could be considered high, but as a negative filter

3. Tuning XG Boost (oversampled)¶

Hyper Parameters

Following XGBoost hyper parameters will be used to tune to increase the model performance.

  1. n_estimators : Number of boosting rounds
  2. scale_pos_weight : Balancing of positive and negative weights.
  3. subsample : Subsample ratio of the training instance
  4. learning_rate : XGB's boosting learning rate
  5. gamma : Minimum loss reduction required to make a further partition on a leaf node of the tree.
  6. colsample_bytree : subsample ratio of columns when constructing each tree.
  7. colsample_bylevel : subsample ratio of columns for each level.
  8. max_depth : maximum depth for base learners

Cross Validation

For cross validation GridSearchCV will be used

  • scoring will be done for precision and F1 score
  • To parallelise n_jobs=-1 will be used
In [1080]:
# Grid of hyper parameters to choose from for GridSearchCV using XG Boost
xgb_parameters = {
    "n_estimators": [100,150,200],
    "learning_rate":[0.1,0.2],
    "subsample":[0.6,0.7,0.9,1],
    "gamma":[0,0.5,1],
    "colsample_bytree":[0.7,0.9,1],
    "colsample_bylevel":[0.7,0.9,1],
    'max_depth': [3, 4, 5]
}

# Precision and F1 scoring used to compare parameter combinations
xgb_scoring = {
    'precision' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.precision_score),
    'f1' : metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)
}

# Create and run the grid search for all above combinations of hyper params
xgb_grid_obj = GridSearchCV(xg_boost_classifier_over,
                        xgb_parameters,
                        scoring=xgb_scoring,
                        refit='f1',
                        cv=5,
                        n_jobs=-1)
xgb_grid_obj = xgb_grid_obj.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

# Set the clf to the best combination of parameters
xg_boost_classifier_tuned = xgb_grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
xg_boost_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[1080]:
XGBClassifier(base_score=None, booster=None, callbacks=None,
              colsample_bylevel=0.9, colsample_bynode=None, colsample_bytree=1,
              device=None, early_stopping_rounds=None, enable_categorical=False,
              eval_metric='logloss', feature_types=None, feature_weights=None,
              gamma=0.5, grow_policy=None, importance_type=None,
              interaction_constraints=None, learning_rate=0.1, max_bin=None,
              max_cat_threshold=None, max_cat_to_onehot=None,
              max_delta_step=None, max_depth=4, max_leaves=None,
              min_child_weight=None, missing=nan, monotone_constraints=None,
              multi_strategy=None, n_estimators=100, n_jobs=None,
              num_parallel_tree=None, ...)

              colsample_bylevel=0.9, colsample_bynode=None, colsample_bytree=1,
              device=None, early_stopping_rounds=None, enable_categorical=False,
              eval_metric='logloss', feature_types=None, feature_weights=None,
              gamma=0.5, grow_policy=None, importance_type=None,

In [1081]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, validation data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(xg_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over, X_val, y_val)
In [1082]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_val_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val
)

# print and compare model performances
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_performance = model_performances(xg_boost_classifier_train_perf, xg_boost_classifier_val_perf)
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_performance
Out[1082]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.773355 0.634594 0.760762 0.691974
Validation 0 0.748835 0.539143 0.646018 0.587762

Model Summary¶

  • Validation performance improved after hyperparameter tuning, especially in precision and F1.

  • Training performance decreased, indicating reduced overfitting.

  • The model still shows mild overfitting, but it is less severe than before.

Importances¶

In [1083]:
# compute and print the important features
xgb_importances = xg_boost_classifier_tuned.feature_importances_
xgb_indices = np.argsort(xgb_importances)
xgb_feature_names = list(X.columns)

# plot the feature importance
plt.figure(figsize=(8,8))
plt.title('XG Boost Feature Importances')
plt.barh(range(len(xgb_indices)), xgb_importances[xgb_indices], color='violet', align='center')
plt.yticks(range(len(xgb_indices)), [xgb_feature_names[i] for i in xgb_indices])
plt.xlabel('Relative Importance')
plt.show()

Observations

  • XG Boost hyper parameter tuned model considers the employee high school as the important feature for applicants getting visas certified. This could be used in negative filter context. This is followed by Job experience, Wages being yearly and Continent Asia

  • It can also be observed the features Job experience, High School Education, Asia continent is in top 6 for all 3 hyper parameter tuned models

7. Compare hyperparameter tuned models¶

Listing the models¶

In [1113]:
# Create the matrix to display the techniques and the models with their performance on training and testing data
hpt_model_names = [
  'Ada Boost oversampled',
  'Ada Boost hyperparameter tuned',
  'Gradient Boost oversampled',
  'Gradient Boost hyperparameter tuned',
  'XG Boost oversampled',
  'XG Boost hyperparameter tuned'
]

hpt_models = pd.concat([
	ada_boost_classifier_over_performance,
  ada_boost_classifier_tuned_performance,
	gradient_boost_classifier_over_performance,
	gradient_boost_classifier_tuned_performance,
	xg_boost_classifier_over_performance,
	xg_boost_classifier_tuned_performance
  ], axis =0, keys = hpt_model_names)

hpt_models
Out[1113]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Ada Boost oversampled Training 0 0.740034 0.538240 0.742906 0.624225
Validation 0 0.734609 0.469719 0.636000 0.540357
Ada Boost hyperparameter tuned Training 0 0.769286 0.642681 0.746894 0.690880
Validation 0 0.742458 0.546529 0.629252 0.584980
Gradient Boost oversampled Training 0 0.769396 0.636513 0.750727 0.688919
Validation 0 0.745156 0.539143 0.637555 0.584234
Gradient Boost hyperparameter tuned Training 0 0.771650 0.634731 0.756823 0.690421
Validation 0 0.748590 0.538405 0.645704 0.587193
XG Boost oversampled Training 0 0.828284 0.707374 0.839323 0.767720
Validation 0 0.730684 0.508863 0.614082 0.556543
XG Boost hyperparameter tuned Training 0 0.773355 0.634594 0.760762 0.691974
Validation 0 0.748835 0.539143 0.646018 0.587762

Observations on hyperparameter models¶

Following are the observations based on the final performance results of the three hyperparameter-tuned, oversampled models (AdaBoost, GradientBoost, and XGBoost),

1. Best Precision (Validation Set):¶

  • XGBoost: 0.646018
  • GradientBoost: 0.645704
  • AdaBoost: 0.629252

Key Observation: Precision is the critical metric for EasyVisa, and XGBoost performs best.

2. Best F1 Score (Validation Set):¶

  • XGBoost: 0.587762
  • GradientBoost: 0.587193
  • AdaBoost: 0.584980

Key Observation: F1 score is the next important metric (precision-recall balance), and XGBoost slightly edges out the others.

3. Recall (Balance Check)¶

  • Ada Boost: 0.546529 (Highest recall)
  • XG Boost: 0.539143 (Close second)
  • Gradient Boost: 0.538405 (Lowest)

Key Observation: XG Boost maintains reasonable recall while optimizing precision

4. Robustness to Overfitting (Gap Analysis):¶

Training vs Validation F1 gap:

  • AdaBoost: 0.690880 → 0.584980 → Gap ≈ 0.106
  • GradientBoost: 0.690421 → 0.587193 → Gap ≈ 0.103
  • XGBoost: 0.691974 → 0.587762 → Gap ≈ 0.104

Key Observation: All models generalize similarly well

5. Higher Baseline Across Metrics (Training):¶

  • XGBoost consistently has higher scores on recall, precision, and F1 on both training and validation. This suggests a more optimized learning fit.

Recommended Final Model for testing¶

Model Recommended: XGBoost (Hyperparameter Tuned) and is selected based on the following key factors.

  • Highest F1 and precision on validation set — both are optimization goals.
  • No overfitting
  • Slight but consistent edge over GradientBoost and AdaBoost.

Performance of final model on test data¶

In [1093]:
# plot confusion matrices for training, testing data
plot_confusion_matrices_v2(xg_boost_classifier_tuned,
                           X_train_over, y_train_over,
                           X_test, y_test,
                           validation_label = "Testing Confusion Matrix")
In [1091]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier_tuned, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

# compute performance metrics on testing data
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_test_perf = model_performance_classification(
    xg_boost_classifier_tuned, X_test, y_test
)

# print and compare model performances
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_final_performance = pd.concat([xg_boost_classifier_tuned_train_perf,
                                                         xg_boost_classifier_tuned_test_perf],
                                                        axis=0,
                                                        keys=["Training", "Testing"])
xg_boost_classifier_tuned_final_performance
Out[1091]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
Training 0 0.773355 0.634594 0.760762 0.691974
Testing 0 0.758438 0.539007 0.669112 0.597054

Model performance summary on test data¶

  • Test F1 (0.597) shows a reasonable generalization from training F1 (0.692).

  • Precision remains strong on test (0.669), indicating good handling of false positives.

  • Recall drops moderately (from 0.635 to 0.539), suggesting some missed positives.

  • Overall, the model generalizes well and is balanced for precision focused

8. Actionable Insights & Recommendations¶

Key Highlights : Business Recommendations¶

  1. Prioritize Job Experience
  2. Implement Strategic Education-Based Filtering
  3. Target Asian Applicants in Strategic US Regions
  4. Favor Yearly Wage Positions for Higher Success Rates
  5. Streamline Operations by Excluding Low-Impact Factors
  6. Deploy Model for Strategic OFLC Workload Reduction

Detailed : Business Recommendations¶

  1. Prioritize Job Experience

    • Use job experience as a primary positive screening filter to identify high-probability candidates
    • Strongly favor applicants with prior job experience as it's the second-highest predictor (0.15 importance) of visa approval
    • Deprioritize candidates without work experience as they show significantly lower approval rates

  2. Implement Strategic Education-Based Filtering

    • Prioritize Bachelor's and Master's degree holders for positive recommendations as they historically show higher approval rates
    • Include Doctorate candidates with moderate priority but focus resources on Bachelor's/Master's applicants first
    • Use High School education as a negative screening filter - high model importance (0.24) to it indicates rejection patterns

  3. Target Asian Applicants in Strategic US Regions

    • Focus recruitment and screening efforts on Asian candidates as they show strong positive correlation (0.06 importance) with approvals
    • Allocate more resources to Asian applicant screening to maximize approval success rates
    • Prioritize applications for West, South, and Northeast regions which demonstrate moderate predictive importance

  4. Favor Yearly Wage Positions for Higher Success Rates

    • Prioritize applications with annual salary structures over hourly/weekly positions (0.07 importance)
    • Use yearly wage as a positive indicator of stable, full-time roles that align with OFLC approval criteria
    • Screen out lower-commitment hourly positions to improve overall approval probability

  5. Streamline Operations by Excluding Low-Impact Factors

    • Remove company size and company age from primary screening criteria as they show minimal predictive power
    • Simplify application review process by focusing only on high-importance features
    • Reduce screening complexity to enable faster, more efficient candidate evaluation

  6. Deploy Model for Strategic OFLC Workload Reduction

    • Implement 67% precision model to pre-filter applications and reduce OFLC manual review burden by ~33%
    • Use two-tier screening approach: automated filtering followed by manual review for edge cases
    • Accept 54% recall limitation while capturing majority of approvable candidates efficiently
    • Monitor model performance and adjust thresholds based on OFLC feedback and approval outcomes

Model limitations and business considerations¶

  1. Model Interpretability and Trust

    • Transparency should be maintained with OFLC stakeholders on key decision factors (education, experience, geography) to ensure accountability
    • Use model as decision support tool rather than replacement for human reviewers, especially for complex or high-stakes cases

  2. Bias and Fairness Concerns

    • Model inherits historical OFLC decision biases which may unfairly impact certain regions, education levels, or wage structures
    • Ensure compliance with labor fairness laws and avoid unintentional discrimination in screening recommendations
    • Regular bias audits needed to maintain equitable treatment across applicant groups

  3. Precision-Recall Trade-offs

    • 67% precision focus reduces false approvals but increases risk of rejecting qualified candidates
    • 54% recall limitation means 46% of good candidates may be missed in automated screening
    • Threshold adjustment flexibility needed based on OFLC capacity and business priorities

  4. Ongoing Model Maintenance and Data Quality Impact

    • Retrain model every 6-12 months to adapt to evolving visa policies, labor market changes, and shifting applicant demographics
    • Feedback loop required for wrongly rejected candidates to improve future model training
    • Prevailing wage inconsistencies and outliers in company data may limit model accuracy
    • Missing work hour context in wage calculations could misrepresent true earning potential
    • Missing valuable predictors like job type, industry sector, and employer credibility that could improve accuracy

Power Ahead
